$comment: Verification of OpenUI Lang's published token-efficiency benchmark. Produced by ./reproduce.sh. All token counts come from OpenUI's own harness (benchmarks/run-benchmark.ts) using their own tokenizer, tiktoken encoding_for_model('gpt-5'), on their own committed samples. No model API calls.

headline.dashboard_note: With the competitor minified, OpenUI Lang is 1.8% LARGER than plain nested JSON (1226 vs 1204) on their flagship dashboard scenario.

A_as_published.description: Their harness, unmodified. Derived arms regenerated from the committed *.oui model output with their own converters. Reproduces benchmarks/README.md verbatim, every cell.

B_minified_competitor.description: Identical to run A with exactly ONE change: the 2-space indent dropped from the C1 converter. Nothing else touched -- same samples, same tokenizer, same harness, same OpenUI arm.
B_minified_competitor.diff_applied: benchmarks/thesys-c1-converter.ts:39-46 -- removed the `null, 2` arguments to JSON.stringify

dashboard.$note: POSITIVE = OpenUI Lang is LARGER. OpenUI loses this scenario.

whitespace_only_minify_of_committed_samp.description: Independent path to the same answer: instead of regenerating through their converter, JSON round-trip the COMMITTED *.c1.json files (a pure whitespace transform). Yields token-for-token IDENTICAL minified totals to run B, proving the minify introduces no semantic change and the committed samples match their current converter.

pristine_pnpm_bench_on_committed_samples.description: Running `pnpm bench` on the pristine checkout WITHOUT regenerating gives a slightly different C1 column than the published table, because the committed samples were reformatted by `prettier --write .` (benchmarks/package.json 'format:fix') after generation. Prettier collapses short arrays onto one line, so the committed files are ~3% smaller than the raw converter output the published table counts. This is a staleness artifact in their repo, not a methodological flaw, and it runs slightly AGAINST them.

competitor_arm_is_pretty_printed.evidence: benchmarks/thesys-c1-converter.ts:39-46 -- return JSON.stringify({ component, error: null }, null, 2)
competitor_arm_is_pretty_printed.note: The other JSON arm, benchmarks/vercel-jsonl-converter.ts:6,10, uses JSON.stringify(...) with NO indent. Two JSON arms in the same benchmark, serialized inconsistently; the pretty-printed one is the arm the headline leads with.

win_collapses_when_minified.note: Research doc was slightly off (see discrepancies below) but directionally correct. Headline falls from -51.7% to -12.1%.

openui_loses_dashboard_when_minified.detail: OpenUI Lang 1226 tokens vs minified C1 JSON 1204 tokens.

token_counter_identical_for_both_arms.evidence: benchmarks/run-benchmark.ts:18-20 defines one countTokens(); applied to all four arms at lines 40-43 with the same encoder instance (line 5).

only_one_arm_is_model_generated.evidence: benchmarks/generate-samples.ts:51-60 makes a single OpenAI call producing OpenUI Lang, written at line 90. Lines 93-102 parse that to an AST and mechanically project it into the other three formats. No rival format is ever generated by a model.
only_one_arm_is_model_generated.note: OpenUI discloses this openly in benchmarks/README.md:18. It is stated, not hidden.

correctness_check_or_repair_loop.evidence: No validate/repair/retry/assert call exists anywhere in run-benchmark.ts or generate-samples.ts. Nothing is rendered, nothing is validated, no failure is recorded, there is no retry.

sample_size.evidence: benchmarks/generate-samples.ts:44 loops over 7 prompts, one streaming call each (line 51), temperature 0 (line 53). No repeats, no variance, no confidence intervals.

latency_table_is_a_tautology.evidence: benchmarks/run-benchmark.ts:16 `const TPS = 60`, lines 83-86 divide token counts by it. Latency is not measured; the 'speedup' column is the token column restated. Real TTFT/TPS exist in samples/metrics.json but are never used in the report.

discrepancies_with_our_research_doc.$comment: The code wins. Where docs/internal/research/rival-formats.md disagrees with the source, these are the corrections.

doc_mislabeled_the_published_baseline.doc_says: C1 as published (pretty) totals 9650, and the published claim is -51.7%
doc_mislabeled_the_published_baseline.code_says: Those two are inconsistent. 9650 is what the PRETTIER-FORMATTED COMMITTED SAMPLES tokenize to (-50.3%). What OpenUI actually publishes (-51.7%) corresponds to the RAW CONVERTER OUTPUT, which totals 9948. The doc conflated the two.
doc_mislabeled_the_published_baseline.impact: Cosmetic labelling error; the pretty-printing allegation is unaffected and if anything the true published baseline is MORE padded than the doc claimed.

doc_minified_counts_are_inflated.doc_says: minified C1 total 5487, dashboard 1207, pricing-page 1378, e-commerce 1363, headline -12.5%
doc_minified_counts_are_inflated.root_cause: The doc's repro used Python json.dumps(), which defaults to ensure_ascii=True and escapes non-ASCII as \uXXXX. The affected samples contain exactly 1 (dashboard '×'), 8 (pricing-page '—') and 3 (e-commerce '•') non-ASCII characters -- matching the +3/+16/+7 token overcounts precisely. Scenarios with zero non-ASCII characters matched exactly. OpenUI's converter uses JS JSON.stringify, which does not escape these.
doc_minified_counts_are_inflated.impact: The doc's error INFLATED the minified competitor, i.e. it erred in OpenUI's favour. Correcting it makes the finding marginally stronger against OpenUI, not weaker.

where_their_methodology_is_better_than_o: Content is held perfectly constant. Because all four arms are projections of one AST, they encode byte-for-byte the same UI. As a measurement of pure serialization density this is a genuinely clean control -- our plan to generate each format independently introduces content variance that we will have to handle with n>1 and CIs. Their design is the stronger one for the narrow question 'how dense is this encoding'; it is simply the wrong design for the question their headline asks.

The report is fully reproducible offline at $0 with no API key: samples are committed, the reporter is deterministic. That is better open-science hygiene than most vendor benchmarks, and better than a harness that requires a key to produce any number at all.
They use the real production tokenizer for the target model rather than a proxy such as character count or a generic BPE.

They disclose the projection design explicitly in benchmarks/README.md:18 ('The LLM always generates OpenUI Lang, then the parsed AST is projected into the other three formats'). The central limitation is stated in their own README, not concealed.
They apply one fairness adjustment IN THE COMPETITOR'S FAVOUR: yaml-converter.ts:8-13 strips information-free empty children arrays from the YAML arm before counting.
reproducibility_defects_found: `pnpm bench` as documented does not work at this commit: benchmarks/ declares `workspace:*` dependencies but is excluded from every glob in pnpm-workspace.yaml, so pnpm skips it (no node_modules, 'tsx: command not found') or fails with ERR_PNPM_WORKSPACE_PKG_NOT_FOUND under --ignore-workspace.

`pnpm generate` is dead at this commit: benchmarks/generate-samples.ts:4 imports '../packages/react-lang/src/parser/parser.js', a path deleted when PR #347 extracted the parser into @openuidev/lang-core. Nobody can regenerate the samples with the committed script.

The published C1 column in benchmarks/README.md does not match what `pnpm bench` prints on a pristine checkout (-51.7% vs -50.3%), because the committed samples were prettier-formatted after the table was written.
